title: Data Drift vs. Concept Drift
concept: Data drift is when the input feature values change over time, while concept drift is when the relationship between features and the target itself changes.

options.text: The gradual drop in accuracy as real-world patterns shift away from training data.
A model automatically becoming faster over time.
A file corruption error during saving.

exercises.explain: Correct! Model decay is accuracy dropping as the world moves on.

cards.title: Two ways a gym class can go wrong
cards.body: A fitness coach designs a workout plan for teenagers. Years later, either 1) the average student's fitness level has changed, so the workout is now too easy or too hard, or 2) the relationship between exercise and results has changed (new equipment makes the same exercise burn fewer calories). These are two completely different problems.

cards.title: Data Drift: The inputs shift
cards.body: Data drift happens when the distribution of input features changes, but the underlying relationship stays the same. For example, average customer income rises over time, but higher income still predicts the same buying behavior it always did.

cards.title: Concept Drift: The rules themselves shift
cards.body: Concept drift is different — it's when the actual relationship between inputs and outputs changes. Imagine a rule 'rain means people buy umbrellas' suddenly breaking because everyone started working from home. Rain no longer predicts umbrella sales the way it used to.

cards.title: Fraud detection example
cards.body: Data drift: transaction amounts generally increase due to inflation, but large transactions are still equally suspicious as before. Concept drift: fraudsters learn to mimic legitimate small purchases, so 'small transaction = safe' stops being a reliable rule.

cards.title: They aren't the same problem
cards.body: It's easy to lump data drift and concept drift together as 'things changed.' But they require different fixes: data drift often just needs retraining on newer data with the *same* rules, while concept drift means the model's fundamental logic itself is now wrong and needs a redesign.

cards.title: Data Drift vs. Concept Drift
cards.body: Two distinct causes of model staleness:

left.text:
Input values shift.
Rules still hold true.
e.g. incomes rise overall.

right.text:
The rule itself changes.
Old logic breaks.
e.g. rain no longer predicts umbrella sales.

options.text: The distribution of input feature values shifts over time, while the underlying relationship stays the same.
The relationship between features and target changes completely.
A server running out of memory.

exercises.explain: Correct! Data drift is about input values shifting while the rule stays valid.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Concept drift means the actual relationship between input features and the target output has changed.
exercises.explain: True! Concept drift means the old rule connecting inputs to outputs no longer holds.

exercises.sentence: When the rule connecting features to the target itself changes, this is called ___ drift.
exercises.explain: Concept drift specifically refers to the relationship (rule) changing.

exercises.prompt: A streaming service notices that 'watching horror movies' used to strongly predict 'will watch a sequel', but after a franchise reboot flopped, that link completely disappeared. What kind of drift is this?

options.text: Concept drift, because the relationship between the input (horror genre) and the output (sequel watching) has broken down.
Data drift, because more users are watching horror movies now.
Neither, this is normal random noise.

exercises.explain: Correct! The underlying rule itself broke, making this concept drift, not just a shift in input volume.

exercises.prompt: An e-commerce model predicts 'high spender' based on age. This year, average customer age has risen from 30 to 40, but older customers still spend the same way older customers always did. What kind of drift is this, and what's the likely fix?

options.text: Data drift — the input distribution (age) shifted, but the rule holds, so simply retraining on newer data should restore accuracy.
Concept drift — the fundamental spending rule has changed and the model needs a full redesign.
Neither — this scenario cannot affect model accuracy.
Data drift, but retraining is useless since drift can never be fixed.

exercises.explain: Exactly! Since the underlying relationship between age and spending is unchanged, this is data drift, and retraining on fresh data (with the same logic) is usually enough to fix it. Next, let's learn how we actually detect these shifts happening in production!
